weeklyStories.headline: The government called dibs on new AI models. You can still use yours on Monday.
weeklyStories.body: The White House told AI companies: 'Let us peek at new stuff 30 days before everyone else.' It's voluntary — like when your friend asks to preview your outfit before you post it. Nobody's grounded. Nobody's losing access. The grown-ups just want a head start.
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: Think of it like a restaurant health inspection — happening in the background, doesn't change your lunch order today. But it tells you the government is paying attention to how powerful these tools are getting. Eventually this shapes what features roll out and when. For now? Carry on.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: The government wants to preview new AI models before they drop. It's voluntary, changes nothing today, but tells you where this is headed.
weeklyStories.preview: The White House told AI companies: 'Let us peek at new stuff 30 days before everyone else.' It's voluntary — nobody's grounded.

weeklyStories.headline: Anthropic's AI is writing code for other AI. No, it's not Skynet. It's more like IKEA.
weeklyStories.body: Anthropic announced Claude is now generating code used to build other AI systems. Before you spiral — this is NOT AI 'becoming sentient' or 'training itself.' It's more like using a power drill to assemble the shelf that will hold more power drills. A tool building better infrastructure for more tools.
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: This is why your AI apps keep getting better every two weeks instead of once a year. The companies building them are using their own AI to speed up the assembly line. For you: expect updates to come faster, new features to land sooner, and the 'cutting edge' to feel more like 'last Tuesday.' The treadmill isn't slowing down — but you don't have to run it. You just need to know what's on it.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: Anthropic is using AI to build better AI. Not the sci-fi kind — the IKEA kind. Using a power tool to build more power tools. It's why everything updates so fast now.
weeklyStories.preview: Anthropic announced Claude is now generating code used to build other AI systems. Before you spiral — this is NOT AI 'becoming sentient.'

weeklyStories.headline: OpenAI shipped a product where humans wrote zero code. They did something harder instead.
weeklyStories.body: An OpenAI team built an entire product using only AI coding agents — no human wrote a single line. But the humans weren't on vacation. They spent their time designing the box the AI worked inside: writing specs, setting rules, defining what 'good enough' looks like. They're calling it 'harness engineering.' We'd call it 'being the creative director on a project where your whole team is AI.'
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: This is Episode 2 at scale. The winning skill isn't doing the work — it's knowing how to describe what you want so clearly that someone (or something) else can execute it. The better your brief, the better the output. Sound familiar? It should. You've been practicing this every time you write a prompt.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: OpenAI built a whole product where AI wrote all the code. The humans? They were the creative directors — specs, constraints, quality bar. The new skill isn't building. It's briefing.

siteLinks.label: Episode 2: Tell Me What You Want

weeklyStories.preview: An OpenAI team built an entire product using only AI coding agents — no human wrote a single line.

weeklyStories.headline: AI did 7,000 tax returns at 97% accuracy. The accountants didn't get fired — they got promoted.
weeklyStories.body: OpenAI and a tax company built a system where AI drafts returns, human accountants review them, and the system learns from their corrections in real time. It saved a third of prep time per return. The accountants' expertise didn't become irrelevant — it became the training data.
weeklyStories.whyYouCare: This is the clearest preview of how AI changes professional work: it does the first draft, you make it right, and your corrections make it smarter next time. Your expertise isn't being replaced. It's being amplified and scaled. The people who know their domain best become the most valuable — because the machine literally learns from their judgment. If that sounds like your job with extra steps, congratulations. You're already prepared.
weeklyStories.cocktailParty: AI did 7,000 tax returns, 97% accuracy. The accountants reviewed and corrected it, and the AI got better from their expertise. Not replaced — amplified. That's the pattern for every professional field.

siteLinks.label: Episode 1: On Wednesdays We Use AI

weeklyStories.preview: OpenAI and a tax company built a system where AI drafts returns, human accountants review them, and the system learns from their corrections in real time.

dailyHeadlines.headline: Google upgraded your AI without asking. You've been using it for a week.
dailyHeadlines.body: Search, Docs, Gmail, Workspace — all quietly switched to Gemini 2.5 (Google's latest model) this week. No notification. No opt-in. No 'would you like to try the new version?' pop-up. It just got better while you weren't looking.
dailyHeadlines.whyYouCare: If you use Google at work, you're already using better AI than you had last Monday. Your Gmail summaries are sharper, your Docs suggestions are smarter, your search understands you better. This is how AI adoption actually happens for most people — not by downloading a new app, but by the apps you already use getting quietly upgraded under the hood. You didn't choose AI. AI chose your workflow.
dailyHeadlines.cocktailParty: Google switched your AI to a better model across Gmail, Docs, and Search. You've been using it all week. Nobody told you. That's how this works now.

siteLinks.label: Episode 1: On Wednesdays We Use AI

dailyHeadlines.preview: Search, Docs, Gmail, Workspace — all quietly switched to Gemini 2.5 this week. No notification. No opt-in.

dailyHeadlines.headline: Apple Intelligence is finally here. Only took them a year and a half of 'just one more thing.'
dailyHeadlines.body: Remember when Apple announced AI everything at WWDC 2025 and then... didn't ship most of it? Well, the iOS 19 beta finally has it all: smart Siri, writing tools in every text field, notification summaries, image generation. The wait is over. The 'one more thing' was patience.
dailyHeadlines.whyYouCare: If you have a recent iPhone, you now have an AI assistant baked into your phone — no app to download, no subscription to pay. Writing help anywhere you type. A Siri that can actually DO things (multi-step tasks, not just 'here's what I found on the web'). Check Settings → Apple Intelligence. The stuff that felt like a tech demo is now just... your phone.
dailyHeadlines.cocktailParty: Apple Intelligence is finally fully live after a year of delays. If you have a recent iPhone, just go to Settings. No app to download — it's built in now.
dailyHeadlines.preview: Remember when Apple announced AI everything at WWDC 2025 and then... didn't ship most of it? The iOS 19 beta finally has it all.

dailyHeadlines.headline: Microsoft wants $30/month per person for AI at work. Your CFO has opinions.
dailyHeadlines.body: Copilot Pro is $30 per seat per month. For a 500-person company? That's $180,000 a year. One CFO called it 'the new Salesforce creep' — the kind of line item that starts small and suddenly it's everyone's biggest software expense. Companies are doing the math, and the math is making them nervous.
dailyHeadlines.whyYouCare: If your company hasn't given you AI tools yet, this is probably why. The per-person cost is making leadership ask: 'Who needs this? How do we measure it? Are people actually using it?' If you want AI tools at work, you need to be ready to answer those questions. What would you DO with it? How would it save time? Episode 2 is your cheat sheet for that conversation.
dailyHeadlines.cocktailParty: Microsoft charges $30/person/month for AI at work. Big companies are sticker-shocked. It's why your IT team hasn't turned it on — and why being able to explain what you'd DO with it matters.

siteLinks.label: Episode 2: Tell Me What You Want
